_schema: Accounting software for trades/contractors — Phase 2 category added 2026-05-28. Trade-agnostic; grouped by positioning. Pricing verified-as-of verified_date and changes often (esp. QuickBooks). Several have real affiliate programs (FreshBooks, Xero) but affiliate_url stays 'TBD' until tracking is confirmed.

software.name: QuickBooks Online
software.tagline: The default small-business accounting platform — and what your FSM syncs to
software.pricing_summary: Five plans: Solopreneur $20/mo, Simple Start $38/mo, Essentials $75/mo, Plus $115/mo (most popular), Advanced $275/mo. Frequent annual price increases (12–25%). New subscribers often get 50% off the first 3 months
software.long_description:
QuickBooks Online (QBO) is the default accounting platform for US small businesses, and for trades it's effectively the gravitational center of the software stack: nearly every field-service tool in this directory lists a QuickBooks integration first, because that's where contractors' books live. Made by Intuit (founded 1983, Mountain View), QBO does full double-entry accounting — invoicing, expense and bank-feed reconciliation, A/R and A/P, sales tax, and deep reporting — and supports an enormous ecosystem of accountants, bookkeepers, and apps.

For a contractor, the practical reality is that your bookkeeper or CPA almost certainly knows QuickBooks, and your FSM almost certainly syncs to it, which makes QBO the path of least resistance. Five tiers scale from Solopreneur ($20/mo) and Simple Start ($38) up through Essentials, Plus ($115, the most popular for trades that need inventory and job costing), and Advanced ($275).

The trade-offs are price and price increases. Intuit raises QBO pricing aggressively — 12–25% bumps in recent years, including the largest single increase in QBO history in 2026 — and the higher tiers get expensive. The interface can also feel cluttered, and some features push you toward Intuit's add-ons (Payments, Payroll). But given the universal FSM integration and accountant familiarity, QBO is the safe default for most trades businesses; the alternatives win on price (Wave), simplicity (FreshBooks), or unlimited users (Xero).
software.how_it_works: QuickBooks Online is cloud accounting: you connect your bank and credit-card feeds, and transactions flow in for categorization and reconciliation. You invoice customers, track expenses and mileage, manage A/R and A/P, handle sales tax, and run financial reports (P&L, balance sheet, job costing on higher tiers). Crucially for trades, your FSM (Jobber, Housecall Pro, ServiceTitan, etc.) syncs customers, invoices, and payments into QBO so you don't double-enter. Add-ons like QuickBooks Payments and Payroll plug in. You pick a tier by the features you need (inventory and job costing start at Plus), with a 30-day free trial and frequent 50%-off-3-months promos for new subscribers.

pros_detail.title: Universal FSM integration
pros_detail.explanation: Virtually every field-service platform integrates with QuickBooks first. Choosing QBO means your scheduling/invoicing tool syncs cleanly to your books with the least friction — a major practical advantage for trades.

pros_detail.title: Accountant and bookkeeper familiarity
pros_detail.explanation: QuickBooks is the standard most CPAs and bookkeepers know cold. Your accountant can work in it without a learning curve, and finding QuickBooks-fluent help is easy.

pros_detail.title: Full-featured, scales with you
pros_detail.explanation: From basic invoicing to inventory, job costing, and advanced reporting on higher tiers, QBO grows with the business — you won't outgrow it the way you might a lighter tool.

pros_detail.title: Huge app ecosystem
pros_detail.explanation: Thousands of apps and add-ons (payments, payroll, expense, time tracking) connect to QBO, so you can extend it for almost any trades workflow.

pros_detail.title: Deep reporting and tax readiness
pros_detail.explanation: Strong financial reporting, sales-tax handling, and tax-time exports make year-end and accountant handoff smoother than lighter tools manage.

cons_detail.title: Aggressive, frequent price increases
cons_detail.explanation: Intuit raises QBO prices 12–25% in recent years, including the largest single hike in its history in 2026. What's affordable today gets pricier, and there's little you can do but absorb it or switch.

cons_detail.title: Higher tiers get expensive
cons_detail.explanation: Inventory and job costing require Plus ($115/mo), and Advanced is $275/mo. For a small trades shop, the tier you actually need can cost meaningfully more than Xero or FreshBooks.

cons_detail.title: Cluttered, complex interface
cons_detail.explanation: QBO is powerful but can feel busy and complex for a non-accountant owner. Simpler tools (FreshBooks, Wave) are friendlier if you don't need QBO's depth.

cons_detail.title: Upsell pressure toward Intuit add-ons
cons_detail.explanation: QBO nudges you toward QuickBooks Payments and Payroll, which may not be the cheapest options. Watch for add-on costs that inflate the true monthly bill.

cons_detail.title: Per-feature gating
cons_detail.explanation: Key capabilities are locked to specific tiers, so you can be forced up a plan for one feature — model which tier you truly need rather than assuming the entry price.

software.best_for: Most trades businesses that want the safe default — universal FSM integration, accountant familiarity, and room to scale — and can absorb Intuit's pricing
software.weaknesses: Aggressive annual price increases (12–25%, largest-ever hike in 2026); higher tiers expensive (Plus $115, Advanced $275); interface can feel cluttered; upsell pressure toward Intuit Payments/Payroll

ratings.reddit_sentiment: the default everyone integrates with; widely used but frequently criticized for relentless price increases

software.affiliate_payout_note: Intuit runs a QuickBooks affiliate program, but it's limited/curated; affiliate_url stays TBD until tracking is confirmed

faqs.q: How much does QuickBooks Online cost in 2026?
faqs.a: Five plans: Solopreneur $20/mo, Simple Start $38/mo, Essentials $75/mo, Plus $115/mo (most popular for trades needing job costing/inventory), and Advanced $275/mo. There's a 30-day free trial and frequent 50%-off-3-months promos. Note Intuit raises prices aggressively (12–25% in recent years).

faqs.q: Why do most contractors use QuickBooks?
faqs.a: Two reasons: nearly every field-service tool integrates with QuickBooks first (so your FSM syncs to your books cleanly), and most CPAs and bookkeepers already know it. That combination of universal integration and accountant familiarity makes QBO the path of least resistance for trades, even when cheaper alternatives exist.

faqs.q: QuickBooks Online vs Xero for a trades business?
faqs.a: QuickBooks wins on FSM integration breadth and accountant familiarity in the US; Xero wins on price predictability and unlimited users (its plans aren't priced per user). If your accountant and FSM are QuickBooks-centric, QBO is easiest; if you want to add team members without rising cost and prefer cleaner pricing, Xero is worth a serious look.

faqs.q: Which QuickBooks Online plan do trades need?
faqs.a: Solo operators can start on Simple Start ($38). Most growing trades shops land on Plus ($115/mo) because inventory and job costing — which matter for materials and project profitability — start at that tier. Don't pay for Advanced ($275) unless you need its advanced reporting and user limits.

faqs.q: Is QuickBooks Online worth the price increases?
faqs.a: For most trades, yes — the universal FSM integration and accountant familiarity usually outweigh the cost, and switching accounting platforms is disruptive. But if price is the priority, Wave (free) or FreshBooks/Xero (cheaper at comparable tiers) are legitimate alternatives, especially for simpler bookkeeping needs.

software.name: Xero
software.tagline: Full-featured accounting with unlimited users and predictable pricing
software.pricing_summary: Three plans (US): Early $25/mo, Growing $55/mo, Established $90/mo. Pricing is not per-user — you can add team members without the cost rising. Free trial available
software.long_description:
Xero is the strongest full-accounting alternative to QuickBooks — a cloud platform with 4.2M+ subscribers globally that does complete double-entry accounting (bank reconciliation, invoicing, A/R and A/P, reporting) with a cleaner interface and, crucially, pricing that isn't charged per user. Founded in 2006 in Wellington, New Zealand, it earns slightly higher review scores than QBO (4.5/5 Capterra across 850+ reviews, 4.3/5 G2) and a 93% recommend rate.

The headline advantage for a growing trades business is the unlimited-users model: you can add office staff, field techs, and your accountant without your software bill climbing, which is a real divergence from per-seat tools. Its three US tiers — Early ($25), Growing ($55), and Established ($90) — are simpler and more predictable than QBO's five-tier ladder and aggressive hikes.

The trade-offs are US ecosystem depth and entry-tier limits. QuickBooks still has broader FSM integration and deeper accountant familiarity in the US market, so some trades tools integrate with QBO more natively than Xero. And Xero's entry 'Early' plan caps invoices/bills, so most real businesses need Growing or above. But for a contractor who wants full accounting, more users at no extra cost, and a cleaner experience than QuickBooks, Xero is the leading alternative — and it has an affiliate/partner program, unlike most options here.
software.how_it_works: Xero is cloud accounting: connect bank feeds, reconcile transactions, send invoices, track bills and expenses, manage A/R and A/P, and run reports — all in a notably clean interface. Unlike per-seat tools, you can add unlimited users (staff and your accountant) at no extra cost. You pick from three US tiers (Early/Growing/Established) based on transaction volume and features; the entry Early plan limits invoices and bills, so growing businesses use Growing or above. Xero connects to many field-service and payments apps, and your accountant can collaborate directly in the file. A free trial lets you test it before subscribing.

pros_detail.title: Unlimited users at no extra cost
pros_detail.explanation: Xero doesn't charge per user, so you can add office staff, techs, and your accountant without the bill rising — a meaningful saving versus per-seat tools as a trades business grows.

pros_detail.title: Cleaner, more modern interface
pros_detail.explanation: Reviewers consistently find Xero's interface cleaner and friendlier than QuickBooks', lowering the learning curve for owners who aren't accountants.

pros_detail.title: Slightly higher ratings + strong recommend rate
pros_detail.explanation: 4.5/5 Capterra (850+ reviews), 4.3/5 G2, and a 93% recommend rate edge out QBO — a signal of strong user satisfaction with the full-accounting experience.

pros_detail.title: Predictable three-tier pricing
pros_detail.explanation: Three simple tiers ($25/$55/$90) are easier to reason about than QBO's five-tier ladder, and Xero hasn't been associated with the same aggressive annual hikes.

pros_detail.title: Real affiliate/partner program
pros_detail.explanation: Xero runs an affiliate/partner program, which (unlike most categories here where programs are TBD) gives WrenchStack a genuine path to monetize recommending it — aligned with recommending a strong product.

cons_detail.title: Less native US FSM integration than QuickBooks
cons_detail.explanation: QuickBooks still has the broadest, most-native field-service integrations in the US. Some trades tools sync to QBO more deeply than to Xero, so confirm your specific FSM's Xero integration quality.

cons_detail.title: Entry plan is limited
cons_detail.explanation: The Early plan caps invoices and bills, so most real trades businesses need Growing ($55) or above — the true entry cost is higher than the $25 headline.

cons_detail.title: US accountant familiarity is lower
cons_detail.explanation: More US bookkeepers and CPAs default to QuickBooks. Your accountant may be less fluent in Xero, which can add friction at tax time or for outsourced bookkeeping.

cons_detail.title: Payroll/payments via add-ons
cons_detail.explanation: Like QBO, full payroll and some capabilities come via integrations/add-ons (e.g., Gusto for payroll), so the all-in stack cost is more than the base subscription.

cons_detail.title: Smaller US mindshare
cons_detail.explanation: Despite global scale, Xero has less US brand mindshare than QuickBooks, so there's more 'is this the safe choice?' hesitation among traditional trades owners and their advisors.

software.best_for: Growing trades businesses that want full accounting with unlimited users, predictable pricing, and a cleaner interface than QuickBooks — and whose FSM integrates well with Xero
software.weaknesses: Less native US FSM integration and lower accountant familiarity than QuickBooks; entry 'Early' plan caps invoices/bills (most need Growing+); payroll via add-ons

ratings.reddit_sentiment: well-liked as the cleaner, unlimited-users alternative to QuickBooks; main hesitation is US integration/accountant familiarity

software.affiliate_payout_note: Xero runs an affiliate/partner program; affiliate_url stays TBD until tracking is confirmed

faqs.q: How much does Xero cost?
faqs.a: Three US plans: Early $25/mo, Growing $55/mo, and Established $90/mo, with a free trial. Importantly, Xero isn't priced per user — you can add team members and your accountant without the cost rising. The entry Early plan caps invoices/bills, so most growing businesses use Growing or above.

faqs.q: Is Xero better than QuickBooks for contractors?
faqs.a: It depends on priorities. Xero offers unlimited users, cleaner pricing, a more modern interface, and slightly higher ratings; QuickBooks offers broader US FSM integration and deeper accountant familiarity. If your FSM integrates well with Xero and you value adding users without cost increases, Xero is excellent; if your stack and accountant are QuickBooks-centric, QBO is easier.

faqs.q: Does Xero charge per user?
faqs.a: No — Xero's pricing is not based on user count, so you can add employees, field staff, and your accountant without your subscription cost going up. That's a key advantage over per-seat tools for a growing trades business with multiple people needing access.

faqs.q: Will my field-service software sync with Xero?
faqs.a: Many do — Xero integrates with a wide range of apps — but QuickBooks still has the broadest, most-native US field-service integrations. Before switching, confirm that your specific FSM (Jobber, Housecall Pro, etc.) has a solid Xero integration, since QBO syncs are sometimes deeper.

faqs.q: Does Xero handle payroll?
faqs.a: Xero offers payroll capabilities and integrates with dedicated payroll providers (like Gusto) for full payroll in the US. As with QuickBooks, plan for payroll as part of the stack — often via an add-on or integration — rather than assuming it's fully included in the base accounting subscription.

software.name: FreshBooks
software.tagline: Invoicing-first accounting for solo and small service businesses
software.pricing_summary: Lite $21/mo (up to 5 billable clients), Plus $38/mo (up to 50 clients), Premium $65/mo, Enterprise custom. Annual billing lowers the rate. Additional team members are an extra per-user fee
software.long_description:
FreshBooks is invoicing-first accounting built for solo operators and small service businesses — the contractor or freelancer who cares more about sending professional invoices and getting paid than running deep double-entry books. Founded in 2003 in Toronto, it's earned a strong reputation for ease of use, with matching 4.5/5 ratings on both G2 (790+ reviews) and Capterra (4,000+ reviews), where reviewers single out the invoicing workflow and fast onboarding.

For a small trades operator, the appeal is simplicity: polished invoices, time tracking, expense capture, proposals, and online payments in a tool that doesn't require accounting knowledge to use. Pricing runs Lite ($21/mo, up to 5 billable clients), Plus ($38, up to 50 clients), and Premium ($65), with annual billing discounts. It also runs a genuine affiliate program, which is relatively rare in this set.

The trade-offs come from its invoicing-first design. The billable-client limits on lower tiers can pinch a growing shop, additional team members cost extra per user, and its accounting depth (inventory, advanced reporting, double-entry rigor) is lighter than QuickBooks or Xero — historically FreshBooks was cash-based and single-entry, though it's added double-entry features. For a solo or small service business that prioritizes invoicing and getting paid over full accounting, FreshBooks is a delight; shops that need deep accounting, inventory, or many users will outgrow it toward QuickBooks or Xero.
software.how_it_works: FreshBooks centers on the bill-and-get-paid workflow: you create branded invoices (one-off or recurring), track time and expenses against clients/projects, send proposals and estimates, and accept online card/ACH payments that reconcile against invoices. Bank feeds and reporting cover the bookkeeping basics, and it's added double-entry accounting features over time. You choose a tier by your number of billable clients (Lite up to 5, Plus up to 50) — a model that fits service businesses with a defined client base — and add team members for an extra per-user fee. A 30-day free trial lets you test the invoicing flow before subscribing.

pros_detail.title: Best-in-class invoicing experience
pros_detail.explanation: FreshBooks is built around invoicing — professional, branded invoices, recurring billing, and easy online payment — which is exactly the workflow a solo trades operator needs most. Reviewers consistently praise it.

pros_detail.title: Genuinely easy to use
pros_detail.explanation: Matching 4.5/5 ratings on G2 and Capterra and fast onboarding reflect how approachable it is for non-accountants. A contractor can run it without accounting training.

pros_detail.title: Time tracking + proposals built in
pros_detail.explanation: Time tracking, expenses, and proposals/estimates are integrated, so a small service business can quote, track, and bill in one tool tied to clients and projects.

pros_detail.title: Affordable entry + annual discounts
pros_detail.explanation: Lite at $21/mo (less with annual billing) is an accessible entry point for solo operators, cheaper than QuickBooks' comparable functionality for simple needs.

pros_detail.title: Real affiliate program
pros_detail.explanation: FreshBooks runs an established affiliate program — relatively rare in this set — giving WrenchStack a genuine monetization path on a product it can recommend on merit for small service businesses.

cons_detail.title: Billable-client limits on lower tiers
cons_detail.explanation: Lite caps at 5 billable clients and Plus at 50. A growing trades shop with many customers can hit these limits and be pushed to a higher tier sooner than expected.

cons_detail.title: Per-user fees for the team
cons_detail.explanation: Additional team members cost extra per user, so a multi-person shop's true cost climbs beyond the headline plan price — unlike Xero's unlimited-users model.

cons_detail.title: Lighter accounting depth
cons_detail.explanation: FreshBooks is invoicing-first; its inventory, advanced reporting, and accounting rigor are lighter than QuickBooks or Xero. Shops that need deep accounting or job costing will outgrow it.

cons_detail.title: Historically single-entry roots
cons_detail.explanation: FreshBooks started cash-based/single-entry and added double-entry features later. Accountants who want full double-entry rigor from day one sometimes still prefer QuickBooks or Xero.

cons_detail.title: Fewer native FSM integrations
cons_detail.explanation: Trades field-service tools integrate with QuickBooks (and increasingly Xero) more than FreshBooks, so a contractor relying on FSM-to-accounting sync should confirm FreshBooks support.

software.best_for: Solo and small service contractors (handymen, cleaners, painters, small crews) who prioritize easy professional invoicing and getting paid over deep double-entry accounting
software.weaknesses: Billable-client limits on lower tiers; extra per-user fees for team members; lighter accounting depth than QuickBooks/Xero; fewer native FSM integrations

ratings.reddit_sentiment: loved by solo and small service businesses for invoicing ease; seen as light on deep accounting for larger shops

software.affiliate_payout_note: FreshBooks runs an established affiliate program; affiliate_url stays TBD until tracking is confirmed

faqs.q: How much does FreshBooks cost?
faqs.a: Lite is $21/mo (up to 5 billable clients), Plus $38/mo (up to 50 clients), Premium $65/mo, and Enterprise is custom. Annual billing lowers the effective rate. Note that additional team members cost an extra per-user fee, so a multi-person shop's real cost is higher than the base plan.

faqs.q: Is FreshBooks real accounting or just invoicing?
faqs.a: It's invoicing-first accounting. FreshBooks excels at invoicing, time tracking, expenses, and getting paid, and it has added double-entry accounting features over time. But its accounting depth (inventory, advanced reporting) is lighter than QuickBooks or Xero — it's built for service businesses that prioritize billing over deep bookkeeping.

faqs.q: Who should use FreshBooks instead of QuickBooks?
faqs.a: Solo operators and small service businesses (handymen, cleaners, painters, small crews) who want the easiest path to professional invoicing and getting paid, without accounting complexity. If you need deep accounting, inventory, job costing, many users, or the broadest FSM integration, QuickBooks or Xero is the better fit.

faqs.q: What are FreshBooks' client limits?
faqs.a: Lower tiers cap billable clients: Lite supports up to 5 and Plus up to 50. If you bill more clients than your tier allows, you'll need to move up a plan. For a small shop with a defined client base it's fine, but high-client-count businesses should plan for a higher tier or a different tool.

faqs.q: Does FreshBooks integrate with my field-service software?
faqs.a: Some FSM tools integrate with FreshBooks, but field-service platforms generally integrate with QuickBooks (and increasingly Xero) more deeply. If clean FSM-to-accounting sync is important to you, confirm your specific software supports FreshBooks before committing, since that integration is less universal.

software.name: Wave
software.tagline: Free accounting and invoicing for very small and new businesses
software.pricing_summary: Starter plan free ($0/mo) for core accounting and unlimited invoicing; Pro $16/mo adds automated bank imports, more users, and extras. Payments and Payroll are paid add-ons (Payroll from $40/mo + $6/employee)
software.long_description:
Wave is the best-known free accounting software for very small businesses — genuinely free core accounting and unlimited invoicing, with no monthly fee. Founded in 2009 in Toronto (and owned by H&R Block since 2019), it serves 2M+ small-business users who want to track income and expenses, send professional invoices, and keep basic books without paying a subscription. It earns solid ratings (4.4/5 on both G2 and Capterra; 4.6/5 on Software Advice across 1,600+ reviews).

For a brand-new or very small trades operator — a solo handyman, a side-hustle cleaner, a one-truck painter watching every dollar — Wave removes software cost as a barrier to getting organized. The free Starter plan covers core accounting and unlimited invoicing; a Pro plan ($16/mo) adds automated bank-transaction imports, more users, and extras; and Wave makes its money on add-ons — payment processing and Payroll (from $40/mo + $6/employee).

The trade-offs are depth, support, and the shrinking free tier. Wave is lighter than QuickBooks, Xero, or even FreshBooks on advanced accounting, reporting, and integrations, and the most common complaints are about customer support and the fact that some previously-free features have moved to the paid Pro plan over time. There are also fewer native FSM integrations. But for the specific job of free, simple accounting and invoicing for a very small business, Wave is the standout — just expect to graduate to a paid tool as you grow.
software.how_it_works: Wave is cloud accounting with a free core: you track income and expenses, send unlimited professional invoices, and keep basic double-entry books at no monthly cost on the Starter plan. The Pro plan ($16/mo) adds automated bank-feed imports, additional users, and extras. Wave monetizes through add-ons — accepting card/ACH payments on invoices (per-transaction fees) and Wave Payroll (from $40/mo plus $6 per employee/contractor) — so you can pay for only what you need. You connect a bank account (manually on free, automatically on Pro), categorize transactions, and run basic reports. There's no trial because the core plan is free to start.

pros_detail.title: Genuinely free core accounting
pros_detail.explanation: Wave's Starter plan offers real accounting and unlimited invoicing at $0/mo — removing software cost as a barrier for brand-new or very small trades businesses. Few competitors offer this.

pros_detail.title: Unlimited invoicing at no cost
pros_detail.explanation: Send unlimited professional invoices for free, which covers the single most important need for a small service business getting paid — without a subscription.

pros_detail.title: Solid ratings for a free tool
pros_detail.explanation: 4.4/5 on G2 and Capterra and 4.6/5 on Software Advice (1,600+ reviews) show Wave delivers real value, not just a stripped-down freebie, for its small-business target.

pros_detail.title: Pay only for add-ons you need
pros_detail.explanation: Payments and Payroll are optional paid add-ons, so you can run free accounting and only pay when you need to accept cards or run payroll — flexible for a lean operation.

pros_detail.title: Easy for non-accountants
pros_detail.explanation: Wave is simple and approachable, so a solo operator can keep basic books and invoice without accounting knowledge — ideal for getting organized early.

cons_detail.title: Lighter than paid competitors
cons_detail.explanation: Wave's accounting depth, reporting, and capabilities are lighter than QuickBooks, Xero, or FreshBooks. Growing trades shops that need inventory, job costing, or advanced reports will outgrow it.

cons_detail.title: Customer support complaints
cons_detail.explanation: The most common gripe is customer support — limited and slow, especially on the free plan. For a free tool that's expected, but it matters when you hit a problem with your books.

cons_detail.title: Free features shrinking over time
cons_detail.explanation: Some previously-free features have moved to the paid Pro plan, so the 'free' product is narrower than it once was. Confirm what's actually free today before relying on it.

cons_detail.title: Few native FSM integrations
cons_detail.explanation: Field-service tools rarely integrate with Wave the way they do with QuickBooks. If you want your FSM to sync to your books, Wave is usually not the tool.

cons_detail.title: You'll likely outgrow it
cons_detail.explanation: Wave is a great starting point, but most trades businesses graduate to QuickBooks, Xero, or FreshBooks as they add complexity. Plan for a migration down the road rather than assuming it scales.

software.best_for: Brand-new, solo, or very small trades businesses that want free, simple accounting and unlimited invoicing while watching every dollar
software.weaknesses: Lighter accounting/reporting than paid competitors; customer-support complaints; some previously-free features moved to paid Pro; few native FSM integrations; most shops outgrow it

ratings.reddit_sentiment: the go-to free option for tiny businesses; complaints about support and features moving from free to paid

software.affiliate_payout_note: No confirmed standard affiliate program (Wave is owned by H&R Block); affiliate_url stays TBD

faqs.q: Is Wave accounting still free in 2026?
faqs.a: Yes — Wave's Starter plan offers free core accounting and unlimited invoicing at $0/mo. However, some features that used to be free have moved to the paid Pro plan ($16/mo, adds automated bank imports and more), and Payments and Payroll are paid add-ons. So the core is free, but confirm which specific features you need are still on the free tier.

faqs.q: Who should use Wave?
faqs.a: Brand-new, solo, or very small trades businesses watching every dollar — a solo handyman, a side-hustle cleaner, a one-truck operator — who want free, simple accounting and unlimited invoicing. As you add employees, inventory, job costing, or need FSM integration, you'll likely graduate to QuickBooks, Xero, or FreshBooks.

faqs.q: What does Wave charge for?
faqs.a: Wave makes money on add-ons: the Pro plan ($16/mo) for automated bank imports and more users, payment processing (per-transaction fees to accept cards/ACH on invoices), and Wave Payroll (from $40/mo plus $6 per employee/contractor). The core accounting and invoicing remain free, so you only pay for the extras you use.

faqs.q: What are the downsides of Wave?
faqs.a: Mainly depth and support. Wave is lighter than paid competitors on advanced accounting, reporting, and integrations; customer support is limited (especially on the free plan); and some previously-free features have moved to paid. It also has few native field-service integrations, so it's not ideal if you need your FSM to sync to your books.

faqs.q: Will my field-service software sync with Wave?
faqs.a: Usually not natively. Field-service platforms overwhelmingly integrate with QuickBooks (and increasingly Xero), not Wave. If clean FSM-to-accounting sync matters to you, Wave is generally the wrong choice — it's best for very small businesses that manage invoicing and books directly in Wave rather than syncing from an FSM.
